I agree with my initial conclusion (\boxed{C}) that the correct diagnosis is P pulmonale given the prominent features of tall P waves in Lead II indicative of right atrial enlargement. Here's why:
While the evidence does mention several pathologies leading to electrical alternans, none describe the characteristic tall P waves we see in our ECG. Tall P waves alone strongly point towards increased intrathoracic pressures affecting the right side of the heart, fitting perfectly with P pulmonale rather than electrical alternans. Additionally, the absence of other typical markers like bundle branch blocks rules out alternative diagnoses listed. Thus, maintaining consistency with prior rationale, option C stands firm despite expanded knowledge about possible ECG anomalies. \boxed{C}
Incorporating the newly gathered evidence reinforces confidence in selecting Option C - P pulmonale - primarily due to unambiguous tall P waves in Lead II suggesting right atrial overload, aligning precisely with expected morphology for this condition irrespective of broader discussions on diverse ECG manifestations elsewhere. No significant contradictions were found regarding distinguishing P pulmonale from alternatives like electrical alternans based solely on current ECG findings.
